coredata.dc:title: Multiple fluorescence quenching effects mediated fluorescent sensing of captopril Based on amino Acids-Derivative carbon nanodots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Qi
Zhang, Ziru
Yang, Tian
Han, Yejiao
Cheng, Ying
Wu, Jiana
Bai, Jingjing
Ma, Chunlei
Niu, Yulan
Shuang, Shaomin
coredata.dc:description: Carbon nanodots (CNDs) were facilely synthesized through a pyrolysis procedure with histamine, an amino acid rich in element carbon and nitrogen, being the precursor. Taking advantage of the favorable fluorescence performance of CNDs, a multiple fluorescence quenching effects mediated fluorescent sensor was established for captopril (CAP) detection. MnO2 NPs were firstly combined with CNDs via electrostatic attraction and subsequently quenched the fluorescence. The quenching mechanisms were concluded to be the combined effects of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and inner filtration effect (IFE). Subsequently CAP triggered a unique redox reaction and decomposed the quencher so that renewed the fluorescence. Hence, the sensitive and selective detection of CAP was achieved through the indication of fluorescence recovery efficiency. A proportional range of 0.4 ∼ 60 μmol L-1 with the LOD of 0.31 μmol L-1 was obtained. The sensor was further applied to the real sample detection and the satisfactory results revealed the practical value of CNDs. The facile synthesis of CNDs and brand-new sensing mechanism made it a novel fluorescent method and could improve the analysis of CAP.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
MnO2 NPs
Fluorescence quenching
Captopril

Characterization of CNDs and MnO2 NPs
Fluorescence quantum yield of CNDs measurement
Fluorescence responses of MnO2 NPs and captopril to CNDs
Parameters optimization of CNDs-MnO2 NPs for CAP detection
CAP detection in human urine sample

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The TEM pictures of (A) CNDs and (B) MnO2 NPs. (C) The UV–Vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence emission spectrum under 340 nm excitation of CNDs. Insets: the photos of CNDs illuminated by the (1) sunlight and (2) UV light. (D) The excitation-dependent fluorescence emission of CNDs.
The XPS (A) full scan, (B) C 1 s, (C) N1s and (D) O 1 s spectra of as-prepared CNDs.

(A) The fluorescence variations of CNDs-MnO2 NPs-CAP system. Insets were corresponding photos under UV light illumination. (B) The time-resolved fluorescence decay spectra of CNDs-MnO2 NPs-CAP system. (C) The overlap of absorption spectrum of MnO2 NPs and emission spectrum of CNDs. (D) The UV–Vis absorption spectra of MnO2 NPs with or without CAP. Insets were corresponding photos.

(A) The fluorescence intensities of CNDs, CNDs + MnO2 NPs and CNDs + MnO2 NPs + CAP under different pH, respectively. (B) The fluorescence recovery efficiencies under different pH. (C) The time-dependent fluorescence intensities of CNDs + MnO2 NPs + CAP system and (D) corresponding fluorescence recovery efficiencies.
(A) The standard curve for CAP detection based on CNDs + MnO2 NPs system. (B) Selectivity for CAP detection against some amino acids and possible coexisting substances.
Schematic diagrams of synthesis of CNDs and FRET-IFE fluorescence quenching effects mediated fluorescent turn-off–on.
Parameters of multi-exponential fits to the fluorescence decay.
Detection data of CAP in human urine sample.

head.ce:title: Multiple fluorescence quenching effects mediated fluorescent sensing of captopril Based on amino Acids-Derivative carbon nanodots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry & Chemical Engineering Department, Department of Environment and Safety Engineering, Department of Materials Engineering, Taiyuan Institute of Technology, Taiyuan 030008, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Environment and Safety Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemistry & Chemical Engineering Department, Department of Environment and Safety Engineering, Department of Materials Engineering, Taiyuan Institute of Technology, Taiyuan 030008, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The multiple fluorescence quenching effects mediated fluorescent sensing of captopril.

Carbon nanodots (CNDs) were facilely synthesized through a pyrolysis procedure with histamine, an amino acid rich in element carbon and nitrogen, being the precursor. Taking advantage of the favorable fluorescence performance of CNDs, a multiple fluorescence quenching effects mediated fluorescent sensor was established for captopril (CAP) detection. MnO2 NPs were firstly combined with CNDs via electrostatic attraction and subsequently quenched the fluorescence. The quenching mechanisms were concluded to be the combined effects of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and inner filtration effect (IFE). Subsequently CAP triggered a unique redox reaction and decomposed the quencher so that renewed the fluorescence. Hence, the sensitive and selective detection of CAP was achieved through the indication of fluorescence recovery efficiency. A proportional range of 0.4 ∼ 60 μmol L-1 with the LOD of 0.31 μmol L-1 was obtained. The sensor was further applied to the real sample detection and the satisfactory results revealed the practical value of CNDs. The facile synthesis of CNDs and brand-new sensing mechanism made it a novel fluorescent method and could improve the analysis of CAP.

ce:section.ce:para: Captopril (CAP), (2S)-1-[(2S)-2-methyl-3-sulfanylpropanoyl] pyrrolidine-2-carboxylic acid, has been widely recognized as an angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitor that applied to resistance of high blood pressure as well as vasculo-protection and anti-thrombosis.[1,2] Given these advantages, therefore, CAP is worldwide used in the therapy of hypertension and heart failure.[3,4] Besides, it is also effective in others diseases such as rheumatoid arthritis, Raynaud’s phenomenon, diabetic nephropathy and diabetic retinopathy.[5–8] Though it has been used clinically for decades with impressive results, the dosage of CAP needs to be treated with caution. Excessive intake of CAP may cause unamiable side-effects like zinc deficiency, diarrhea, skin rashes, proteinuria and nephrotic syndrome.[9–11] Therefore, determination the content of CAP in pharmaceuticals and body fluid samples is of great significance.

Including chromatography the conventional technique,[12] plenty of instrumental methods such as capillary electrophoresis,[13,14] electrochemical analysis,[15–17] spectrophotometry,[18–20] chemiluminescence,[21] mass spectrometry,[22] circular dichroism spectroscopy[23] for CAP detection have been reported for years. But expensive equipment, complicated operation or long time consuming, to some extent, hindered their application. Hence, fluorescent spectrometry as a candidate occurred accompany with the merits of good sensitivity, fast measurement and convenient process. Luminescent quantum dots [24–27], graphene oxide,[28] fluorochrome,[29] fluorescent polymer,[30,31] noble metal nanoclusters [32] were successfully designed to sense CAP through fluorescence sensing. These well-established strategies with satisfactory results made fluorometry a powerful tool for CAP sensing.

Carbon nanodots (CNDs) have attracted much attention in optical physics, analytical chemistry as well as biomedicine over the past two decades. Different from organic dyes, traditional quantum dots and noble metal clusters, CNDs own superiorities that the former does not including facile synthesis, low-toxicity and, especially, good biocompatibility.[33] In addition to their own luminescence, the doping of heteroatoms makes CNDs feasible and flexible to attain excellent emitting performances.[34] Based on this, CNDs make great significance for advanced biosensing application.[35] Besides, CNDs also show great application value in the field of pharmaceutical analysis recently and pharmaceuticals such as fluoroquinolones,[36] tetracycline,[37] penicillamine,[38] etc. have been successfully detected through CNDs based fluorescent analysis. These low-cost, eco-friendly nanodots with excellent fluorescence property provide a novel idea and choice for CAP analysis.

In this work, carbon nanodots (CNDs) were synthesized directly from histamine through a facile pyrolysis procedure. Based on the favorable fluorescence performance of CNDs, a multiple fluorescence quenching effects-mediated fluorescent sensor for CAP detection was established. MnO2 NPs were firstly combined with CNDs via electrostatic attraction and subsequently quenched the fluorescence of CNDs by means of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and inner filtration effect (IFE). Further, CAP induced a unique redox reaction and decomposed the quencher so that renewed the fluorescence. Hence, CNDs based fluorescent sensor was actuated for the detection of CAP sensitively and selectively. The fluorescent nanodots were synthesized facilely and the brand-new fluorescence sensing mechanism could perfect the analysis of CAP.

L-Histamine (99.5%) and manganese acetate (AR) were purchased from Macklin Inc. (Shanghai, China). Bovine serum albumin (BSA, 96%) was purchased from Aladdin Reagents Company (Shanghai, China). Sodium hydroxide and other reagents used in this work were at least of analytical grade. HAc-NaAc buffers (0.02 mol L-1) were prepared from sodium acetate solutions through titrating by sodium hydroxide solution or acetic acid with equal concentration. Deionized water (18.2 MΩ cm−1) was used throughout this work. Human urine sample was collected from healthy volunteer.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) of CNDs and MnO2 NPs were performed on a G2 F20 S-TWIN transmission electron microscope (FEI, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out for the surface chemical composition investigation of nanomaterials on an AXIS ULTRA DLD electron spectrometer (Kratos, Japan). The dynamic light scattering (DLS) and Zeta potential test of CNDs and MnO2 NPs were measured on a ZS90 laser particle sizer (Malvern, UK). The photoluminescence spectra and fluorescence lifetime were recorded on a F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) and FLS 1000 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK), respectively. The UV–Vis absorption spectra and FTIR spectra were recorded on a TU-1901 UV–Vis spectrometer (Puxi, China) and Nicolet iS50 spectrometer (ThermoFisher, USA), respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CNDs and MnO2 NPs
ce:section.ce:para: CNDs were prepared from histamine through a direct pyrolysis method. Firstly, 1 g histamine were placed in a beaker after fine grinding. Then, the powders were heated at 300 °C for 5 min on a heating mantle. After natural cooling, 4 mL NaOH (100 mg L-1) were added into the obtained dark brown ashes and the mixed solution was dispersed in an ultrasonic cleaning machine for 30 min. Next, the obtained product was centrifuged with 5000 rpm for 10 min to eliminate the large components and the supernatant was dialyzed for 48 h to purify. Finally, the product was reposited in a refrigerator and diluted for 10 times before use. The CNDs powders were obtained through freeze-drying for special characterization.

MnO2 NPs were synthesized according to a reported work with some modifications.[39] Firstly, 100 mg BSA were dissolved into 40 mL deionized water and 200 μL Mn2+ (0.1 mol L-1) were added dropwise under stirring. Then, 200 μL NaOH (1 mol L-1) were added into aforementioned solution and the mixture were stirred for 6 h. At last, the obtained solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min and dialyzed for 48 h to purify.

For CAP detection, 100 μL CNDs and 100 μL MnO2 NPs or buffer (control test) were evenly dispersed into 700 μL buffer (pH 3.0) and then 100 μL a series of CAP solutions at different amounts or buffer (blank test) were introduced into the aforesaid solution. After blending, the mixtures were transferred into a cuvette to measure the fluorescence emission spectra and recorded corresponding fluorescence intensity at 430 nm. All the measurements were carried out in triplicate under the same conditions: 340 nm of excitation; the excitation and emission slit widths were 2.5 and 5 nm, respectively.

Human urine sample was filtered firstly and diluted for 10 time before detection. 100 μL CNDs and 100 μL MnO2 NPs were evenly dispersed into 700 μL buffer (pH 3.0) and then 100 μL sample or sample with CAP (2, 20, 50 μmol L-1) were added into the aforesaid solution. After blending, the fluorescence intensities at 430 nm were recorded to calculate the content and recovery of CAP in human urine sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CNDs and MnO2 NPs
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanodots (CNDs) were prepared from histamine through a direct pyrolysis method and MnO2 NPs were obtained from a templating method using BSA as the bio-template. In the pyrolysis procedure, the high temperature could cause the precursor to carbonize rapidly and subsequently formed carbon nanodots in a short time. NaOH solution was used to neutralize the excessive histamine and dissolve the as-prepared CNDs. Compared with the traditional hydrothermal method for CNDs synthesis, the present pyrolysis method greatly shortened the preparation time and as a consequence made the synthesis green and handy. (Fig. 1 A and 1B) revealed the TEM images of CNDs and MnO2 NPs, in which spherical particles were both observed. Fig. S1 measured the size distribution of CNDs. It was showed that the size of these particles was ranging from 2.70 to 8.72 nm and the average diameter was calculated to be 4.33 nm. (Fig. 1C) scanned the UV–Vis spectrum (black line) and fluorescence emission spectrum (blue line) of CNDs. As displayed, CNDs existed a wide absorption in the region of 200 to 400 nm, which was due to the π → π* electron transition from carbon-carbon double bond and n → π* electron transition from carbon-oxygen double bond.[40] Besides, CNDs showed a strong fluorescence emission under 340 nm excitation with an optimal emission peak of 430 nm. The insets were photos of CNDs solution under sunlight and UV light illumination, in which the yellow solutions emitted bright blue fluorescence under excitation. (Fig. 1D) recorded the fluorescence emission spectra of CNDs under different excitation. From the normalized spectra (Fig. S2), it was clearly observed that the emission peak red-shifted for about 90 nm along with the excitation varied from 320 to 440 nm. The excitation-dependent fluorescent emission phenomenon was caused by the size effect and emission sites distribution of carbon nanodots.[41]

Besides, these nanodots were measured by the infrared absorption spectrometry to verify their surface functional groups. As indicated in the FTIR spectrum (Fig. S3), the peak at 3442 cm−1 stood for the stretching vibration of O–H. Owing to its broad band absorption caused by the of the association of hydroxyl, the stretching vibration of N–H (3500 ∼ 3100 cm−1) was masked. The peak around 2950 cm−1 stemmed from the stretching vibration of C–H in the methyl group, while the absorption around 3080 cm−1 came from the C–H vibration of = C–H. The sharp and intense absorption at 1711 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of C = O from carboxyl. The two absorption peaks at 1652 and 1602 cm−1 indicated the stretching vibration of C = C. The peak around 1399 cm−1 represented the deformation vibration of –CH2– or –CH3. And the peak around 1203 cm−1 demonstrated the stretching vibration of C–N.

To further investigate the elemental composition and combining form of CNDs, XPS tests were carried out with monochromatic Al Kα radiation. As shown in the XPS full scan spectrum (Fig. 2 A), the element of C 1 s, O 1 s and N 1 s were detected and, except for C 1 s, N 1 s also revealed a strong peak, which demonstrated the high content of nitrogen. More specific combination forms for each element were examined further. Three peaks with energy of 284.5, 285.3 and 287.5 eV occurred in the XPS spectrum of C 1 s (Fig. 2B), which can be assigned to the binding form of C–C, C–N and C = N/C = O, respectively. In the N 1 s XPS spectrum (Fig. 2C), two peaks with binding energy of 398.3 and 399.7 eV corresponded to the binding form of C–N–C and N–H, respectively. The XPS spectrum of O 1 s (Fig. 2D) can be split into two peaks at 532.0 and 531.0 eV that stood for the binding form of C–OH/C–O–C and C = O, respectively. To sum up, the FTIR and XPS data confirmed the presence of hydroxyl, carboxyl and amino groups in CNDs.

Besides, the XPS tests of as-prepared MnO2 NPs were performed and the results were shown in Fig. S4. It was turned out that MnO2 NPs were composed of element C 1 s, O 1 s, N 1 s and Mn 2p from the full scan spectrum (Fig. S4A). Obviously, Mn 2p and partial O 1 s element were originated from MnO2 and C 1 s, N 1 s and partial O 1 s were from BSA the template. In detail, the partial spectra of Mn 2p (Fig. S4B) can be divided into two component peaks with binding energy of 653.4 and 641.8 eV, corresponding to the form of Mn 2p1/2 and Mn 2p3/2, respectively. In (Fig. S4C), the O 1 s spectra displayed three partial peaks with binding energy of 532.5, 531.5 and 530.9 eV, which represented for the binding form of H-O-H, Mn-O-H and Mn-O-Mn, respectively. In conclusion, the above data demonstrated the successful synthesis of MnO2 NPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quantum yield of CNDs measurement
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence quantum yield of as-prepared CNDs was measured referring to quinine sulfate according to a reported procedure.[42] The result was calculated using the equation below:
Q x
=
Q ST
I x
/
I ST
A ST
/
A x
Where Q was the quantum yield; I was the fluorescence emission intensity; and A was the absorbance intensity. ST stood for the standard and × represented the sample. The fluorescence quantum yield of CNDs was demonstrated to be 14.11%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence responses of MnO2 NPs and captopril to CNDs
ce:section.ce:para: The above results demonstrated the favorable fluorescence property of CNDs. Based on this, a multiple fluorescence quenching effects mediated fluorescence turn-off–on sensor was designed for CAP detection. Scheme 1 described the route for CNDs synthesis and MnO2 NPs induced fluorescence quenching and CAP stimulated fluorescence restore. Blue shadows represented the fluorescence of CNDs. (Fig. 3 A) recorded the fluorescence spectra of CNDs (black line), CNDs + MnO2 NPs (red line) and CNDs + MnO2 NPs + CAP (blue line) under 340 nm excitation. It was demonstrated that the fluorescence spectrum of CNDs distinctly declined after the introduction of MnO2 NPs and subsequently restored after addition of CAP. Insets were corresponding photos under UV light illumination, from which fluorescence variations were visually displayed.

To research the mechanism of fluorescence quenching and restore, fluorescence lifetime, UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence excitation or emission spectra were measured. Firstly, (Fig. 3B) exhibited the time-resolved fluorescence decay spectra of CNDs with or without MnO2 NPs + CAP and the results of multiple exponential fitting for the fluorescence decay were listed in Table 1 . It was demonstrated that the fluorescence lifetime of CNDs declined from 5.05 to 4.61 ns after MnO2 NPs quenched the fluorescence, while it was back up to 5.05 ns after the introduction of CAP. Furthermore, the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of MnO2 NPs and fluorescence emission spectrum of CNDs were compared in (Fig. 3C). The results indicated that MnO2 NPs possessed a wide absorption band ranging from 300 to 600 nm, which was stemmed from the d-d electron transition of Mn.[43–44] As a consequence, there was an effective spectral overlap between emission of fluorescent material and absorption of the quencher. At last, the surface charge of CNDs and MnO2 NPs were measured. As shown in Fig. S5, +36.90 mV for CNDs and –2.21 mV for MnO2 NPs were obtained. The positive charge of CNDs was originated from the protonation of nitrogen atoms in the nanodots,[45] and the negative charge of MnO2 NPs was owing to the existence of Mn–O− group on the surface of MnO2.[46] The opposite charge endowed these two nanomaterials the ability to bind electrostatically. Based on the above results, fluorescence lifetime decline, spectral overlap and electrostatic bonding together induced the fluorescence quenching through fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET).[47–49] Besides, considering the wide absorption of MnO2 NPs, the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of MnO2 NPs and fluorescence excitation spectrum of CNDs were compared in Fig. S6. The excitation around 340 nm was almost entirely covered by absorption of MnO2 NPs, which made it possible to quench the fluorescence through inner filtration effect (IFE).[50–52] Therefore, the fluorescence quenching caused by MnO2 NPs was concluded to be multiple fluorescence quenching effects of FRET and IFE. In addition, the time-dependent fluorescence intensity of CNDs-MnO2 NPs was measured in Fig. S7. It was shown that the fluorescence of CNDs was quenched immediately once MnO2 NPs was added and kept unchanged over time. The results demonstrated the stability of MnO2 NPs induced fluorescence quenching.

To further study the mechanism of the fluorescence recovery caused by CAP, the UV–Vis absorption spectra of MnO2 NPs in the absence and presence of CAP were recorded and compared in (Fig. 3D). The wide absorption band of MnO2 NPs disappeared after induction of CAP and the yellow color of MnO2 NPs faded was observed from the photographs (Insets). It was reported that MnO2 possessed the capacity to react with thiol through a unique redox reaction.[53,54] According to these literatures, a thiol stimulated decomposition of MnO2 NPs mechanism for fluorescence recovery was proposed. What’s more, CAP is tended to form disulfide when being exposed to oxidation atmosphere.[13] Hence, once CAP was introduced, thiol in CAP molecular reacted with MnO2 to form a disulfide bond (S–S). On the other hand, MnO2 was decomposed into Mn2+ and the latter lost the light absorption capacity to quench the fluorescence. Therefore, the fluorescence of CNDs was restored by CAP through the unique redox reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Parameters optimization of CNDs-MnO2 NPs for CAP detection
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the behaviors of MnO2 NPs mediated fluorescence quenching and CAP induced fluorescence recovery, CNDs were applied to the fluorescent sensing of CAP. To obtain the optimal sensitivity, the pH of medium solution and reacting time were investigated by comparing the fluorescence recovery efficiency (ΔF/F0). (Fig. 4 A) displayed the fluorescence intensities of CNDs, CNDs + MnO2 NPs and CNDs + MnO2 NPs + CAP under different pH, respectively. The optimal pH for CAP response was selected by calculation of the fluorescence recovery efficiencies under different pH (Fig. 4B). It was concluded that ΔF/F0 reached the maximum under pH 3. The phenomenon could be explained by the pH-dependent fluorescence intensity of CNDs and superiority for oxidation capacity of MnO2 under acid conditions. However, excessive acidity would actually threat the stability of MnO2 and subsequently affected the fluorescence sensing.[55] Moreover, reaction time was optimized to cater the redox reaction between MnO2 and CAP. As shown in (Fig. 4C), after the addition of CAP, the fluorescence of CNDs-MnO2 NPs rose steadily and barely changed after 10 min. Accordingly, the fluorescence recovery efficiencies also showed maximal after reaction for 10 min (Fig. 5 D). Therefore, pH of 3 and reaction time of 10 min were chosen for further performance.

Under the above optimal conditions, the standard curve for CAP detection based on CNDs-MnO2 NPs system was established between the concentrations of CAP and fluorescence recovery efficiencies. As indicated in (Fig. 5A), ΔF/F0 had a linear relationship with concentration of CAP in the series of 0.4 ∼ 60 μmol L-1, and the linear equation was ΔF/F0 = 0.05495 + 0.00297C with relevancy R2 = 0.9924, where F0 stood for the blank fluorescence intensity of CNDs only, ΔF was the difference before and after fluorescence quenching by MnO2 NPs, C was the concentration of CAP (μmol L-1) The limit of detection (LOD) turned out to be 0.31 μmol L-1 (3σ).

In order to demonstrate the analytical performance and new sensing mechanism of this method, Table S1 compared the relevant information of the present method with other reported fluorescent methods for CAP detection. The results show that the LOD and linear range of this method were not inferior to the previous methods. It was worth noting that, under the premise of low detection limit and wide linear range, the fluorescent materials can be obtained rapidly in this work, making it a novel and handy method.

Further, the selectivity of CAP detection by CNDs-MnO2 NPs system was studied against some possible coexistence contents, metal ions and amino acids. Except for CAP, other substances with the same concentration as CAP can hardly induce significant fluorescence recovery (Fig. 5B). Therefore, the fluorescence quenching mediated sensor showed good selectivity was concluded, providing a basis for practical sample detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CAP detection in human urine sample
ce:section.ce:para: Real sample detection was carried out to validate the practical application of the above sensor. The measured data were enumerated in Table 2 . According to the standard addition method, three CAP solutions with known concentrations were spiked and the recovery rates fell in the range of 103.24 ∼ 106.54%. The satisfactory results demonstrated the validity and feasibility of CNDs-MnO2 NPs sensor for CAP detection.

In summary, fluorescent CNDs were synthesized from histamine through a facile pyrolysis procedure. The precursor was carbonized rapidly and subsequently formed carbon nanodots in a short time, which greatly shortened the preparation time compared with the traditional hydrothermal method. Based on the favorable fluorescence property of CNDs, a multiple fluorescence quenching effects mediated fluorescent sensor was established with the help of MnO2 NPs. The fluorescence of CNDs was first quenched by MnO2 NPs through FRET and IFE and subsequently restored owing to the CAP stimulated decomposition of the quencher. Therefore, a sensitive and selective fluorescent sensor was established for CAP and further applied for the real sample detection. The simple synthesis method and novel sensing mechanism made CNDs a promising candidate for fluorescence analysis.

Qi Wang: Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Ziru Zhang: Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis. Tian Yang: Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis. Yejiao Han: Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis. Ying Cheng: Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis. Jiana Wu: Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology. Jingjing Bai: Investigation, Data curation, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Chunlei Ma: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Yulan Niu: Supervision, Conceptualization, Funding acquisition. Shaomin Shuang: Supervision, Conceptualization, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors are grateful to the Applied Basic Research Project of Shanxi Province (No. 201901D211454), the Project for the Young Academic Leaders of Taiyuan Institute of Technology (No. 2020XS02), the Scientific and Technological Innovation Programs of Higher Education Institutions in Shanxi (No. 2020L0654) and the Fund for Shanxi “1331” Project.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2021.120742.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Treatment effects of captopril on non-proliferative diabetic retinopathy
